essence: A regulated, asset-heavy, capital-intensive infrastructure business - often a natural monopoly - where returns are largely set by regulators, not markets.
Returns hinge on the regulated asset base and allowed returns (T&D) and on plant load factor + fuel cost + tariffs (generation).
Stable, defensive, dividend-like in regulated segments; merchant generation is more market/price-exposed.
The energy transition (renewables, storage, grid modernisation) is reshaping generation and demanding huge grid investment.
In India, distribution (discom) financial health is the chronic weak link; renewables + grid are the growth story.

definition.oneLiner: Companies that generate, transmit, and distribute electricity (and sometimes water/gas) - largely regulated infrastructure providing an essential service.
definition.jobToBeDone: Deliver reliable, affordable electricity (and other utilities) to homes and businesses - always on, at a regulated price.

valueChain.profitPools: Profit pools are regulated and steady in transmission & distribution (returns on the asset base) and in long-contracted generation; merchant generation is price-exposed; renewables earn long PPA-based returns. The weak link (esp. India) is distribution, where under-recovery destroys value.
valueChain.powerShift: Value is shifting from centralised thermal generation -> renewables + storage + grid modernisation -> distributed/decentralised energy and demand flexibility, as decarbonisation and electrification reshape the system.

unitEconomics.unitOfRevenue: Revenue per unit of electricity (per kWh) sold or wheeled; or regulated return on the asset base
unitEconomics.revenueDrivers: Capacity x Utilisation. Generation revenue = capacity x plant load factor x tariff; T&D revenue = a regulated return on the asset base; tariffs and PLF are the levers.
keyKPIs: Regulated return on equity / asset base

costStructure.shape: Dominated by huge fixed capital (plants, grid) recovered over decades, plus fuel cost (for thermal); regulated segments earn a set return on capital, so the asset base and allowed return drive profit, while fuel and losses are the swing factors.
costStructure.leverage: Regulated returns make it stable but capped; in generation, plant load factor and fuel cost drive margin; the killer is distribution losses (AT&C) and unpaid bills, which can wipe out the chain's economics.

competitionAndMoats.concentration: T&D are natural monopolies (regulated); generation more competitive; India: NTPC (generation giant), Power Grid (transmission), state discoms, and large private players (Adani Power, Tata Power, JSW Energy) + booming renewables IPPs.

structuralForces.regulation: Very heavy - tariffs, allowed returns, licensing, and renewable-purchase obligations are all regulator-set; regulation is the primary determinant of profitability.
structuralForces.cyclicality: Defensive (electricity demand is essential and grows with GDP), though merchant generation is price-cyclical and demand has seasonal peaks.
structuralForces.substitution: Rooftop solar, captive/distributed generation, and storage erode grid demand at the margins; energy efficiency dampens growth.
structuralForces.supplierBuyerPower: Fuel suppliers (coal/gas) and equipment makers hold some power; regulated tariffs limit pricing to buyers; discom financial weakness (a buyer-power/credit issue) plagues the chain in India.

aiPenetration.maturity: Medium - AI in grid management, forecasting, and predictive maintenance; growing with renewables/storage.
aiPenetration.valueChainImpact: AI is increasingly essential to integrate intermittent renewables (forecasting, grid balancing, storage optimisation) and to cut distribution losses/theft and improve reliability - directly attacking the chain's biggest value leaks.
threatsDisruption: Distributed energy + storage bypassing the grid

ranges: Regulated RoE: set by regulator (e.g. ~15-16% in India historically)
AT&C losses (India distribution): high (~15%+), targeted lower
Renewables tariffs: fallen sharply, now very competitive
benchmarks.note: DIRECTIONAL - rules of thumb, not audited figures. Verify before quoting.

valueLevers: Grow the regulated asset base (T&D capex)
Improve plant load factor & fuel efficiency

keyTerms.PLF: Plant Load Factor - capacity utilisation of a power plant.
keyTerms.T&D: Transmission & Distribution - the grid and last-mile delivery.
keyTerms.Discom: Distribution company - sells power to end users (often financially weak in India).
keyTerms.AT&C losses: Aggregate Technical & Commercial losses - power lost to leakage/theft/non-payment.
keyTerms.PPA: Power Purchase Agreement - a long-term contract to buy electricity at a set price.
keyTerms.Asset base / RAB: The regulated capital on which a utility earns its allowed return.
keyTerms.Merchant power: Electricity sold at market prices (not contracted).
keyTerms.RPO: Renewable Purchase Obligation - mandated renewable share.

questionsToAskFirst: What's the regulated return and asset-base growth?
Distribution: AT&C losses and receivables health?
Renewables vs thermal mix and trajectory?
rookieTraps: Treating regulated and merchant economics alike
Underrating the renewables cost crossover (stranded thermal)
Missing that returns are regulator-set, not market-set

whatsUniqueHere: Power & utilities is the rare business where the regulator, not the market, sets your profit: returns are largely a function of how much regulated capital you deploy and the allowed return on it - stable and bond-like, but capped. The art is deploying capital into the asset base and (in India) plugging the chronic value leak of distribution losses and unpaid bills - all while the energy transition rewrites generation economics and demands enormous new investment in grids and storage.

indiaLandscape.marketStructure: A large, growing, structurally power-hungry market: NTPC dominates generation, Power Grid the transmission backbone, state-owned discoms handle distribution (chronically loss-making), and private players (Tata Power, Adani Power/Green, JSW Energy) plus a booming renewables sector are scaling fast; reform focus on discom health and the green transition.
indiaLandscape.note: Semi-perishable - players & policy specifics refresh periodically.

currentState.note: PERISHABLE - refresh via research before quoting. Figures approximate.
currentState.marketStructure: India power: demand at record highs (growth + cooling + electrification); aggressive renewables build-out alongside continued thermal for baseload; grid and storage investment ramping; distribution reform (RDSS) tackling discom losses; Adani Green/Tata Power and others scaling renewables; AI entering grid management.
